modules.title: Welcome — a calm scalp changes everything
modules.html:
Welcome — a calm scalp changes everything

Flakes, itch, tightness — a troubled scalp is exhausting, and most advice for Type 4 hair makes it worse, not better.

Here is the good news: the vast majority of dandruff is manageable once you understand what is actually causing it. This course walks you from knowing your type of flaking, to the real causes, to a simple scalp routine and DIY treatments that soothe — and it is honest about the moment you should see a doctor.

Do one lesson, apply it, come back. Validate each to unlock the next.

Read this first Your scalp is skin. Treat it like the skin on your face — cleanse it, don’t smother it — and half the battle is already won.

modules.title: Dry or oily? Know your flakes
modules.html:
Dry or oily? Know your flakes

The single most useful thing you can do is figure out which kind of dandruff you have — because the two need opposite care.

Dry flakes

Small, white, powdery. Scalp feels tight and itchy, worse in cold/dry air or after harsh shampoo. Often just a dry, irritated scalp.

Oily flakes

Larger, yellowish, greasy, sometimes stuck in patches. Scalp can look red or shiny. Usually linked to Malassezia, a natural scalp yeast (seborrheic dandruff).

Why it matters

Dry flaking wants gentle, hydrating care. Oily/yeast flaking wants regular cleansing and anti-fungal care — and richer oils can make it worse. Guess wrong and you fuel the problem. Look closely (a phone photo of your parted scalp helps) before you treat.

Not sure? Many scalps are a mix, and it shifts with seasons. Start gentle, watch how your scalp responds over two weeks, and adjust — Lesson 3 explains the causes behind each.

modules.title: The real causes (yes, including a yeast)
modules.html:
The real causes (yes, including a yeast)

Dandruff is rarely about “dirty” hair. Here is what’s usually really going on.

  • Malassezia yeast. It lives on everyone’s scalp; in some people it irritates the skin and speeds up flaking. This is the most common cause of persistent, oily dandruff.
  • A dry, irritated scalp. Cold air, hard water, harsh sulfates and over-washing strip the skin barrier → tight, flaky, itchy.
  • Product buildup. Type 4 hair is washed less often (rightly), but gels, heavy butters and dry shampoo pile up on the scalp and trap flakes.
  • Not cleansing enough / too long between washes. Oil + sweat + product feed the yeast.
  • Sensitivity to a product (fragrance, an ingredient) — contact irritation that looks like dandruff.

Notice what is not on the list: “dirty hair”. Dandruff is a skin-balance issue, not a hygiene failure.

The Type 4 twist Because we wash less and often grease the scalp, buildup + yeast is an extremely common combo for us. That’s exactly what the next lesson tackles.

modules.title: The scalp-oiling myth
modules.html:
The scalp-oiling myth

Greasing the scalp every day is one of the most loved — and most misunderstood — Type 4 habits.

Oil does not “feed” or moisturise your scalp skin — only water hydrates. And on an oily or yeast-related scalp, a daily layer of heavy oil does something worse: it feeds the Malassezia and traps flakes, making the itch and flaking worse.

When oil helps

A truly dry, tight scalp can be soothed by a light oil (like jojoba) occasionally — as comfort, not a daily habit.

When oil hurts

An oily/flaky/itchy scalp gets worse with grease. Here you want to cleanse and treat the yeast, not add more oil on top.

So the rule isn’t “never oil” — it’s match the scalp you actually have (Lesson 2). If in doubt on a flaky, itchy scalp: less oil, more gentle cleansing.

Reframe Feed your lengths with oil if they’re dry — that’s where sebum can’t reach. Your scalp makes its own oil; it usually needs cleansing and balance, not more grease.

modules.title: Cleanse right — wash without stripping
modules.html:
Cleanse right — wash without stripping

The foundation of a calm scalp is regular, gentle cleansing that reaches the skin — not just the hair.

1

Part hair in sections and focus shampoo on the scalp, not the lengths.

2

Use the pads of your fingers (never nails) to gently massage — this lifts flakes and boosts circulation.

3

Choose a gentle, sulfate-free cleanser; for oily/yeast dandruff, an anti-dandruff wash (see Lesson 6) 1–2×/week.

4

Rinse thoroughly — leftover product is buildup waiting to happen.

5

Condition the lengths (not the scalp) to keep hair soft.

Dry, flaky scalp

Don’t over-wash. A gentle cleanse every 5–7 days, lukewarm (not hot) water, and skip harsh clarifiers that strip you further.

Oily, yeasty scalp

Wash more often, not less — every 3–5 days — and let an anti-dandruff wash sit 3–5 min so the active can work before rinsing.

Stretching wash day Type 4 hair loves long gaps between washes — but a flaring scalp doesn’t. During a breakout, cleanse the scalp more often even if you protect the lengths.

modules.title: The actives that actually work
modules.html:
The actives that actually work

For stubborn or oily dandruff, gentle washing alone isn’t enough — you need something that targets the yeast or the flaking.

Over-the-counter (fastest, evidence-backed)

  • Ketoconazole or zinc pyrithione — anti-fungal, target Malassezia.
  • Selenium sulfide — reduces flaking and yeast.
  • Salicylic acid — lifts and dissolves stuck flakes.
  • Coal tar — slows skin cell turnover (strong; can dry Type 4 hair).

Use a medicated wash as directed (often 2×/week), let it sit a few minutes, then deep-condition the lengths so the medicine doesn’t leave hair parched.

Gentle exfoliation

Once a week, a scalp scrub or a soft brush lifts dead skin and buildup so treatments can reach the scalp. Don’t scrub raw or broken skin.

DIY actives (natural)

Tea tree and neem have real anti-fungal properties — always diluted (Lesson 8). Helpful for mild cases.

When to level up

If DIY isn’t enough after 2–3 weeks, move to an OTC anti-fungal wash. There’s no shame in it — it’s what works.

Rotate, don’t overload Alternate a medicated wash with your gentle cleanser. Daily strong actives can irritate; consistency 2×/week beats intensity.

modules.title: Soothe & balance the scalp
modules.html:
Soothe & balance the scalp

Once you’re cleansing and treating, the last piece is calming the skin and keeping its pH and moisture balanced — without grease.

  • Rebalance pH with a diluted apple cider vinegar rinse (Lesson 8) — helps buildup, itch and shine.
  • Hydrate lightly if the scalp is dry: a water-based scalp spritz (aloe + water), not a heavy butter.
  • Don’t scratch. It breaks the skin and worsens flaking. Massage with finger pads instead.
  • Protect the barrier: lukewarm water, gentle products, satin at night (cotton wicks moisture and rubs the scalp).

Lifestyle matters more than you’d think

Stress, sleep and diet all influence how reactive your scalp is. You don’t need to be perfect — but a flaring scalp often calms when stress drops and sleep improves.

Dry scalp

Lighter, less-frequent cleansing + a little aloe/water spritz. Avoid alcohol-heavy products that tighten the skin.

Oily scalp

Keep cleansing regular, use the ACV rinse, and resist re-greasing. Balance comes from removing excess, not adding richness.

Small and steady A calm scalp is built with small, consistent habits — not one intense treatment. Two good weeks beats one heroic wash day.

modules.title: Your anti-dandruff DIY treatments
modules.html:
Your anti-dandruff DIY treatments

Four gentle, effective recipes for mild-to-moderate flaking. Always patch-test on your inner arm first, and never apply essential oils undiluted.

1 · Tea tree soothing rinse

To 250 ml water add 3–5 drops tea tree oil + 1 tsp aloe. After cleansing, pour over the scalp, massage 1 min, leave 2–3 min, rinse. Anti-fungal + calming. 1–2×/week.

2 · Neem scalp infusion

Simmer a handful of neem leaves (or 1 tsp neem powder) in 2 cups water 10 min, cool, strain. Use as a final rinse or dab on the scalp with cotton. Traditional anti-fungal/antibacterial. 1×/week.

3 · Apple cider vinegar balance rinse

1–2 tbsp ACV in 1 cup water. After shampoo, pour over scalp, leave 1–2 min, rinse cool. Rebalances pH, lifts buildup, calms itch. Every 1–2 weeks (smell disappears when dry).

4 · Rhassoul & aloe cleansing mask

Mix 2 tbsp rhassoul clay + water (or aloe juice) to a paste. Apply to scalp, leave 5–10 min, rinse well. Gently draws out oil and buildup without stripping. 1×/week for oily scalps.

Dry scalp

Favour the aloe rinse and neem; go easy on clay and ACV (can be drying). Follow with a light aloe/water spritz.

Oily scalp

The rhassoul mask + ACV rinse are your friends. Keep tea tree in rotation; skip heavy oils entirely.

Safety first Patch-test everything. Essential oils always diluted. Stop immediately if anything stings, burns or worsens — and see Lesson 9.

modules.title: When to see a doctor
modules.html:
When to see a doctor

Most dandruff is manageable at home. But some scalp problems are medical — and trying to DIY them wastes time and can make things worse.

See a doctor or dermatologist if you have:

  • Flaking that doesn’t improve after 3–4 weeks of consistent, correct care.
  • Painful, red, swollen patches, or plaques that ooze, crust or bleed.
  • Thick, silvery scales (possible psoriasis) or greasy yellow crusts spreading to face/ears (seborrheic dermatitis).
  • Round, scaly patches with hair loss, or bald spots (possible fungal infection / other conditions).
  • Intense itch that disrupts sleep, or any sudden, severe change.

These can be seborrheic dermatitis, psoriasis, eczema or a scalp infection — all very treatable, but usually with the right medicated or prescription care, not kitchen recipes.

This is not medical advice This course shares general education for everyday dandruff. Your scalp is unique — when in doubt, a professional look is always worth it. Seeking help is smart, not dramatic. 💛

modules.title: Your 4-week scalp reset + cheat sheet
modules.html:
Your 4-week scalp reset

A gentle plan to calm a flaky scalp and build the habit. Adjust to whether your scalp reads dry or oily.

  • Week 1 — Diagnose & clean: identify dry vs oily (Lesson 2). Do a clarifying or ACV rinse once, then start gentle regular cleansing. Photograph your scalp.
  • Week 2 — Treat: add an active 2×/week (tea tree/neem DIY, or an OTC anti-fungal wash for oily/yeast). Stop daily scalp oiling.
  • Week 3 — Soothe: keep cleansing + treating; add exfoliation once, and a light aloe spritz if dry. Satin at night, no scratching.
  • Week 4 — Assess: compare with your Week 1 photo. Better? Lock in the routine. No better? Book a doctor (Lesson 9).

Cheat sheet — “my scalp is…”

…flaky & tight (dry)

Gentler, less frequent washing; lukewarm water; aloe spritz; ease off clarifiers and alcohol.

…flaky & greasy (oily)

Cleanse more often; anti-fungal 2×/week; rhassoul + ACV; stop scalp oils entirely.

…itchy but not flaky

Could be product sensitivity — simplify products, patch-test, rinse well. Persisting? See a doctor.

…flaky after a new product

Likely irritation — stop that product for 2 weeks and watch.

You’ve got this A healthy scalp is a habit, not a cure you buy once. Come back to this course whenever your scalp flares with the seasons. 💛
